coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Hypocrella bambusae for bimodel fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging-guided synergistic photodynamic/photothermal therapy of cancer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Jia, Qingyan
Zheng, Xiuli
Ge, Jiechao
Liu, Weimin
Ren, Haohui
Chen, Shiqing
Wen, Yongmei
Zhang, Hongyan
Wu, Jiasheng
Wang, Pengfei
coredata.dc:description: As phototheranostic agents, carbon dots (CDs), have recently drawn considerable attention due to their excellent physicochemical properties. However, the complex synthetic route and high-cost of CDs greatly limit their practical application. To address this issue, given their nearly infinite supply from nature, Hypocrella bambusae is used as the precursor for the preparation of CDs in this study. The obtained Hypocrella bambusae CDs (HBCDs) possess good water solubility, broad absorption (350–800 nm), red-light emission (maximum peak at 610 nm), and low biotoxicity. Moreover, HBCDs can highly generate 1O2 (0.38) and heat (27.6%) under 635 nm laser irradiation. These excellent properties of HBCDs capacitate them to be utilized for bimodal fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging-guided synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT)/photothermal therapy (PTT). This work provides a new candidate for tumor treatment with the combination of PDT and PTT, and explores a novel approach for the preparation of CD-based phototheranostic agents with natural biomass as raw carbon sources.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Photodynamic therapy
Photothermal therapy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of HBCDs derived from hypocrella bambusae for bimodel FL/PA imaging and synergistic PDT/PTT of cancer.

(a) TEM image of HBCDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of HBCDs. (c) FTIR spectra of HBCDs. (d) XPS survey spectrum of HBCDs. C 1s (e), N 1s (f), O 1s (g) XPS spectrums of HBCDs. (h) Dispersed stability of HBCDs in physiological solutions.

(a) Absorption spectrum of HBCDs in aqueous solution. (b) Emission spectra of HBCDs in aqueous solution. (c) ESR spectra of HBCDs in aqueous solution under 635 nm laser irradiation. (d) Absorption spectrum of DPBF in DMF irradiated by 635 nm laser in the presence of MB. (e) Absorption spectrum of DPBF in DMF irradiated by 635 nm laser in the presence of HBCDs. (f) Decay curves of the DPBF absorption at 416 nm in (d) and (e). (g) Temperature elevations of HBCD aqueous solutions as the irradiation time of 635 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2). (h) IR thermal images of HBCD aqueous solutions after 10 min irradiation of 635 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2). (i) Temperature elevation of HBCD aqueous solution under 635 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2) irradiation/cooling cycle. (j) Plot of cooling time vs the negative natural logarithm of the temperature driving force from the cooling stage in (i).

(a) FL imaging of HeLa cells treated with HBCDs. (b) FL images of DCF-DA staining HeLa cells. (c) Relative viabilities of HeLa cells treated with HBCDs post various treatments. (d) FL images of Calcein AM/PI co-staining HeLa cells. Data are expressed as means ± s.d. (n = 5).

(a) In vivo FL imaging of mice post-i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS. (b) FL intensities of tumors in (a). (c) Ex vivo FL imaging of tumor and major organs at different time points post-i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS. (d) FL intensities of major organs and tumor in (c). (e) In vivo PA imaging of mice post-i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS. (f) PA intensities of tumors in (e). Data are expressed as means ± s.d. (n = 3).

(a) IR thermal images of mice post different treatments. (b) Temperature curves of tumor during the irradiation. (c) Photographs of mice post different treatments. (d) The growth curves of tumor during different treatments. (e) H&E-stained slices of tumor post different treatments. Data are expressed as means ± s.d. (n = 5).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Hypocrella bambusae for bimodel fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging-guided synergistic photodynamic/photothermal therapy of cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials and CityU-CAS Joint Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices, Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100190, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Photochemical Conversion and Optoelectronic Materials and CityU-CAS Joint Laboratory of Functional Materials and Devices
Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As phototheranostic agents, carbon dots (CDs), have recently drawn considerable attention due to their excellent physicochemical properties. However, the complex synthetic route and high-cost of CDs greatly limit their practical application. To address this issue, given their nearly infinite supply from nature, Hypocrella bambusae is used as the precursor for the preparation of CDs in this study. The obtained Hypocrella bambusae CDs (HBCDs) possess good water solubility, broad absorption (350–800 nm), red-light emission (maximum peak at 610 nm), and low biotoxicity. Moreover, HBCDs can highly generate 1O2 (0.38) and heat (27.6%) under 635 nm laser irradiation. These excellent properties of HBCDs capacitate them to be utilized for bimodal fluorescence/photoacoustic imaging-guided synergistic photodynamic therapy (PDT)/photothermal therapy (PTT). This work provides a new candidate for tumor treatment with the combination of PDT and PTT, and explores a novel approach for the preparation of CD-based phototheranostic agents with natural biomass as raw carbon sources.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) has been used as one clinical anticancer modality because of its characteristics of the minimum invasion, selective treatment, few side effects, and low drug resistance [1–3]. When exposed to specific light irradiation, photosensitizers (PSs) can effectively convert surrounding oxygen into reactive oxygen species (ROS) to cause serious oxidative damage of tumor tissues [4–6]. However, the anticancer efficacy of PDT is severely limited by rapid oxygen depletion in hypoxic tumor due to the oxygen-dependence of PDT [7–9]. Moreover, many drawbacks of currently used classical PSs (e.g., photofrin, porphyrin, hematoporphyrin, and phthalocyanine), including complex synthetic procedure, poor water solubility, unfavorable photostability, weak absorption in the therapeutic window, and limited cancer specificity, seriously restrict their further application in cancer treatment [10–13]. Accordingly, aside from significant efforts to develop new PSs with ideal intrinsic characteristics for efficient PDT, the synergistic model with PDT and photothermal therapy (PTT) has also been considered, because the photothermal agent can highly produce hyperthermia from light energy to kill cancer cells, including hypoxic ones, without relying on oxygen in the PTT process [14–18]. To date, although the bifunctional PDT/PTT modality has demonstrated more excellent therapeutic effects compared with PDT alone, most of these methods usually integrate PSs with photothermal agents in one nanoplatform, thereby risking the leakage of therapeutic agents into systemic circulation post-intravenous (i.v.) injection [19–22]. Therefore, a novel and simplex phototherapeutic agent that can generate singlet oxygen (1O2) and hyperthermia for synergistic PDT/PTT in hypoxic tumor is urgently needed.

At present, carbon dots (CDs) have been receiving considerable attention in biomedicine for various applications, such as bio-imaging, drug delivery, and phototherapy, owing to their aqueous solubility, good photostability, easy modification, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [23–30]. To meet the above needs, various precursors including chemical reagent (e.g., citric acid [31], phenylenediamine [32], 4-vinylphenylboronic acid [33], glutathione [34], petroleum coke [35], and polythiophene phenylpropionic acid [36]) and natural biomass (e.g., garlic [37], spinach [38], sesame [39], corn bract [40], crab shell [41], egg whites [42], and bee pollens [43]) have been widely explored. Recently, we have prepared CDs with polythiophene benzoic acid as precursor and demonstrated their excellent synergistic PDT/PTT efficacy for cancer treatment [44]. However, the complex synthetic route and high-cost of polythiophene benzoic acid greatly limited their practical application. In comparison with the chemical reagents, the aforementioned biomass materials derived from nature have been used as the raw carbon sources for preparation of CDs due to their unique advantages, including renewability, environment friendly, low cost, abundant supply, waste utilization, and large heteroatom containment [45–47]. Therefore, we hypothesized that simple and low-cost CDs could be prepared from appropriate natural biomass for potential cancer treatment.

Hypocrella bambusae (HB), a parasitic fungus in bamboo, is commonly used as traditional Chinese medicine for treating rheumatoid arthritis, gastric diseases, and skin diseases [48,49]. Hypocrellins extracted from HB have been proven to possess photodynamic antiviral and anticancer activities [50,51]. With these unique characteristics, we successfully prepared the CDs with HB as raw carbon source by solvothermal method (Fig. 1 ). The obtained HBCDs showed good water solubility, broad absorption, red-light emission, and excellent biocompatibility. Moreover, the HBCDs could be utilized as bimodal fluorescence (FL)/photoacoustic (PA) imaging agents to guide cancer treatment. Furthermore, it’s demonstrated that the HBCDs could efficiently generate 1O2 and hyperthermia for synergistic PDT/PTT with significant improvement of anticancer efficacy compared with singlet PDT modality. As far as we know, this work is the first to design and prepare CDs with natural biomass as raw carbon source for bifunctional FL/PA imaging and synergistic PDT/PTT of tumor.

Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM), Roswell Park Memorial Institute (RPMI)-1640 medium, Fetal bovine serum (FBS), streptomycin (100 μg mL−1), penicillin (100 μg mL−1), Calcein AM, and Propidium iodide (PI) were purchased from Gibco. 1,3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) and 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCF-DA) were purchased from J&K technology co., Itd. All the reagents are analytically pure.

A total of 20 mg HB powder was dissolved in 40 mL of dimethyl formamide (DMF). After ultrasound of 10 min, the solution was treated at 150 °C for 12 h with a Teflon autoclave. HBCDs were obtained by filtration with a filter paper (Millipore, 0.22 µm) and dialysis against ultrapure water (Mw: 3500 Da).

The mixture of HBCD aqueous solution (50 μg/mL) with the 1O2 trapper (2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperide, TEMP) or ∙OH trapper (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-Noxide, DMPO) was irradiated by 0.1 W/cm2 635 nm laser. The ESR spectras were then recorded on a Bruker E500 spectrometer.

The 1O2 quantum yield of HBCDs was measured with DPBF (1 mg/mL) as 1O2 trapping agent. The absorption of HBCD in DMF at 635 nm was fixed to 0.10 OD and then the mixture of 2 mL HBCD solution and 30 μL DPBF solution was irradiated by 635 nm laser (0.1 W/cm2). The decay rate of DPBF at 416 nm was plotted versus irradiation time, and the first-order exponential (tHBCDs) was obtained. The methylene blue (MB) solution with 1O2 quantum yield of 0.52 was used as the control. The 1O2 quantum yield of HBCDs was obtained using Eq. (1).
(1)
ϕ HBCDs
=
ϕ MB
t MB
t HBCDs

HeLa cells were cultured with HBCDs (150 μg/mL) for 6 h and then washed by PBS buffer. Afterwards, the cells were cultured with DCF-DA (1 mg/mL, 2 μL) followed by 635 nm laser irradiation at the low power density of 0.1 W/cm2. The FL images were collected with confocal microscope (Nikon C1si) at 488 nm excitation.

To analyze the photothermal performance, different concentrations of HBCD aqueous solutions were irradiated by 0.8 W/cm2 635 nm laser with a thermocouple probe submerged in the solutions to monitor the temperature. Infrared (IR) thermal images were collected with a IR camera (Ti400).

The absorption of HBCD aqueous solution at 635 nm was fixed to 0.20 OD. Then, 0.8 mL of HBCD solution was irradiated by 0.8 W/cm2 635 nm laser for 10 min, followed by removing the irradiation to restore to ambient temperature. The photothermal conversion efficiency of HBCDs was obtained with Eq. (2).
(2)
η =
h S
(
T max
-
T
surr
)
-
Q
dis
I ( 1 -
10
- A 635
)

HeLa cells and 4T1 cells (donated by the Center of Cells, Peking Union Medical College) were cultured in DMEM or RPMI-1640 medium, respectively, containing 10% FBS, streptomycin (100 U/mL), and penicillin (100 U/mL) at 37 °C and 5% CO2.

HeLa cells seeded in confocal dishes were cultured with HBCDs in culture medium (150 μg/mL) for 6 h and then washed by PBS buffer. The FL images were collected using confocal microscope at 635 nm excitation.

After incubation with various concentrations of HBCDs in culture medium (0, 20, 50, 100, and 150 μg/mL) for 6 h, HeLa cells were irradiated by 635 nm laser (0, 0.1, or 0.8 W/cm2) for 10 min in N2 atmosphere to simulate the hypoxic tumor microenvironment. After incubation of another 12 h, 20 μL 5 mg/mL MTT solution were added to each well and then incubated for 4 h. The culture medium was removed, and 80 μL of dimethylsulfoxide was added. The absorbance at 570 nm was measured to evaluate the cell viabilities.

In addition, after incubation with HBCDs in culture medium (150 μg/mL) for 6 h, HeLa cells were irradiated by 0.1 or 0.8 W/cm2 635 nm laser for 10 min in N2 atmosphere followed by co-staining with calcein AM and PI. The FL images were then obtained by confocal microscope at 488 nm excitation.

The female nude mice (15–20 g) were purchased from Institute of Process Engineering, Chinese Academy of Science. All the animal experiments conformed to the guidelines of China Committee for Research and Animal Ethics. To build tumor model, the mice were subcutaneously injected with 50 μL 4T1 cells (2 × 106) in PBS. The tumor sizes were allowed to reach approximately 75 mm3.

The FL images were obtained at different time point post-i.v. injection of 200 μL HBCDs in PBS (2 mg/mL) with a CRI Maestro 2 in vivo imaging system. After i.v. injection of HBCDs, the tumor and major organs were obtained at 24 h and then imaged. Moreover, the PA images were obtained using a PA tomography system (MSOT invision128) with the same experimental procedure.

The tumor-bearing nude mice were randomly assigned into five groups (n = 5, each group) as follows: (1) without any treatment; (2) i.v. injection of 200 μL HBCDs in PBS (2.0 mg/mL); (3) irradiation with 635 nm laser (0.8 W/cm2, 10 min); (4) i.v. injection of 200 μL HBCDs in PBS (2.0 mg/mL), followed by 635 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W/cm2, 10 min); and (5) i.v. injection of 200 μL HBCDs in PBS (2.0 mg/mL), followed by 635 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2, 10 min). After different treatments, the sizes of tumor and body weights of mice were counted every two days.

The tumors and major organs of HBCDs-treated mice were obtained and fixed in 4% formalin solution. After embedded in paraffin blocks, the samples were sectioned into 4 μm slices and mounted onto glass slides for H&E staining. Then, an optical microscope was used for the pathology of different tissues.

In this work, we first synthesized the HBCDs using HB powder as the precursor through solvothermal method [52,53]. Then, we systematically characterized the morphology and composition of HBCD. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that the spherical HBCDs were successfully prepared with a diameter of 4.76 ± 0.43 nm, which is smaller than the hydrodynamic diameter of 25.6 nm in the HBCD aqueous solution (Fig. S1a) due to the solvent coating layer existed on the surface of HBCDs [54,55]. Additionally, minor changes in size distributions of HBCDs in physiological solutions were observed, which attributed to the non-specific absorption of proteins on the surface of HBCDs [56,57]. Furthermore, a clear fringe spacing of 0.21 nm ascribed to the (1 0 0) facet of graphene was discovered in the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) [58–60]. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was then processed to study the functional groups of HBCDs surface (Fig. 2c). The peak at 3423 cm−1 was attributed to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 1643 cm−1 and 1037 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO, respectively. The peak at 1405 cm−1 was ascribed to the CN stretching vibration [61–63]. In addition, the element composition of HBCDs was further characterized by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) technique. The typical peaks of C 1s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and O 1s (532 eV) with atomic percentages of 73.48%, 4.33%, and 22.19%, respectively, are shown in Fig. 2d. To analyze the surface states of HBCDs, high-resolution XPS spectra are shown in Fig. 2e–g. Four peaks at 284.7, 285.6, 286.3, and 288.0 eV, corresponding to CC, CN, CO, and CO, were observed in high-resolution C 1s spectrum, respectively (Fig. 2e) [64,65]. In Fig. 2f, the high-resolution N 1s spectrum confirmed the presence of pyrrolic N (399.5 eV) and graphite N (400.2 eV) atoms, thereby indicating that N was successfully doped in the HBCDs. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) had two peaks located at 531.1 eV and 532.7 eV, assigned to CO and CO, respectively. All the FTIR and XPS results demonstrated the existence of NH2, OH, and COOH on the surface of HBCDs. The abundance of carboxyl and hydroxy on the surface of HBCD make it exhibited the negative zeta potential with −15.5 mV in water, −14.3 mV in PBS, −13.1 mV in DMEM, and −12.8 mV in FBS (Fig. S1b). In addition, the HBCDs showed excellent stability in physiological mediums without apparent precipitation (Fig. 2h) and significant changes of DLS (Fig. S2a) and surface potential (Fig. S2b) observed even prolonging time to 7 d.

The optical properties of the HBCDs in aqueous solution were subsequently investigated using absorption spectrum and emission spectra. Two absorption peaks at 450 nm and 580 nm derived from the hypocrellins in HB were observed (Fig. 3 a) [66]. In addition, the HBCDs exhibited a slightly excitation-dependent emission with emission peak of approximately 610 nm (Fig. 3b), which is similar to other previously reported CDs; moreover, this emission endowed the HBCDs with excellent capability of FL imaging [44]. To verify the photodynamic activity, the ESR technique was applied to monitor the ROS produced by HBCDs with TEMP and DMPO as 1O2 trapper or ∙OH trapper, respectively [15]. As shown in Fig. 3c, the 1O2 signal from TEMP-1-oxyl evidently increased after 10 min of 635 nm laser irradiation. However, no evident characteristic of ∙OH signal was observed. Furthermore, the 1O2 quantum yield of HBCDs in DMF was assessed with DPBF and MB as 1O2 trapping agent and standard PS (1O2 quantum yield of 0.52), respectively [67]. When the MB (Fig. 3d) and HBCDs (Fig. 3e) existed, the absorbance intensity of DPBF continuously decreased as the time of 635 nm laser irradiation was prolonged. The 1O2 quantum yield of HBCDs was measured to be 0.38 from the decay curves of the DPBF absorption at 416 nm (Fig. 3f).

Next, we also investigated the photothermal performance of HBCDs in aqueous solution under irradiation of 0.8 W/cm2 635 nm laser (Figs. 3g and S3). After 10 min irradiation, the temperature of HBCD aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) increased by 26.9 °C. However, the temperature of water only increased by 5.4 °C. In addition, the IR thermal images of HBCD aqueous solutions further verified that the HBCDs could effectively transfer light to heat (Fig. 3h). Subsequently, the photothermal conversion efficiency (η) of HBCDs was further calculated by a temperature cycle of heat-up and cooling (Fig. 3i and j). ηHBCDs was approximately 27.6%, which is comparable to those of classical photothermal agents, including gold nanorods (21.0%) and cyanine dyes (26.6%) [68,69]. In addition, when irradiated by 0.1 W/cm2 635 nm laser, the temperature of HBCD aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) only increased by 3.7 °C (Fig. S4), which indicated that the photothermal effect could be neglected. From these results, the HBCDs could generate 1O2 only for PDT at the low power of 0.1 W/cm2 and produce 1O2 and heat for simultaneous PDT/PTT at the power of 0.8 W/cm2.

The FL image of HeLa cells was collected to study the cellular uptake behavior of HBCDs (Fig. 4 a). After incubating the HeLa cells with HBCDs for 6 h, bright red fluorescent signals were observed in the cytoplasm, which indicated a successful internalization of HBCDs in the cells. DCF-DA was then used as a green fluorescent indicator to verify the intracellular 1O2 generation of HBCDs [18,70]. It was found that the HeLa cells treated with HBCDs or DCF-DA alone and then irradiated by 635 nm laser showed negligible luminescence (Fig. 4b). By contrast, after co-incubation with HBCDs and DCF-DA and then irradiation by 635 nm laser, a strong green FL of HeLa cells was observed, which indicated the effective production of 1O2 by the HBCDs in the cells. The standard MTT assay was appiled, after validating the excellent 1O2 generation capability of HBCDs, to assess the cytotoxicity and anticancer efficacy of HBCDs in vitro (Fig. 4c). Without 635 nm laser irradiation, the HeLa cells treated with HBCDs showed high cell viability (>90%), even the concentrations of up to 200 µg/mL, demonstrating the low cytotoxicity of HBCDs. Conversely, when the cells incubated with HBCDs were irradiated by 0.1 W/cm2 635 nm laser in N2 atmosphere (PDT group), the cell viabilities gradually decreased with the increase of HBCD concentration. However, approximately 30% viability of cell was observed even at 200 µg/mL of HBCDs because of the weak PDT efficacy in the hypoxic environment. Noticeably, at the power density of 0.8 W/cm2 (PDT + PTT group), high mortality rates were obtained, and approximately 99% of cells died at 200 µg/mL of HBCDs, indicating the better anticancer efficacy of synergistic PDT/PTT compared with PDT alone. Calcein AM/PI co-staining assay was also performed to confirm the synergistic PDT/PTT efficacy of HBCDs in vitro [71–73]. No cell death was found when HeLa cells incubated with HBCDs or irradiated by 0.8 W/cm2 635 nm laser alone in N2 atmosphere (Fig. 4d). However, a part of cells were killed in the PDT group and all cells were completely destroyed in the PDT + PTT group. These findings were consistent with the MTT results. Therefore, the HBCD could be utilized as a potential bifunctional phototherapy agent for synergistic PDT/PTT in hypoxic tumor.

Subsequently, we investigated the in vivo biodistribution of HBCDs using a bimodal FL/PA imaging technique with a combined characteristic of high sensitivity and excellent spatial resolution [36,74]. After i.v. injection of HBCDs, the FL images of mice were collected at different time intervals (Fig. 5 a and b). The FL signals of HBCDs in tumor rapidly increased over time and then reached the maximum at 8 h; thereafter, the signal intensity gradually decreased. Then, the tumor and major organs of mice at different time points post i.v. injection of HBCDs were excised and then imaged. As shown in Fig. 5c and d, the HBCDs could efficiently accumulate in the tumor sites by the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect and exhibited a higher fluorescence intensity compared with the other organs (heart, spleen, and lung) [75–77]. Although the liver and kidney showed high accumulation of HBCDs, their remaining HBCDs gradually decayed over time and were completely removed at 14 day post-i.v. treatment, reflecting that HBCDs might be effectively metabolized from mice [78]. PA imaging was also employed to investigate the in vivo feasibility of HBCDs. After i.v. injection of HBCDs, a clear tumor microstructure with deep tissue-imaging penetration and high spatial resolution is shown in Fig. 5e. The mean signal intensities of tumors (Fig. 5f) also illustrate that the HBCDs could rapidly increase within 8 h post-i.v. injection, which conforms to the above FL imaging results. Accordingly, 8 h post-i.v. injection of HBCDs was regarded as the optimum point for subsequent 635 nm laser irradiation. In summary, HBCDs possess the capability for in vivo bimodal FL/PA imaging-guided phototherapy.

The in vivo phototherapeutic efficacy of HBCDs was further researched given their excellent in vitro anticancer efficacy and efficient in vivo accumulation in tumor. For treatments, five groups of mice were investigated as follows: (1) without any treatment; (2) i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS; (3) 635 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2); (4) i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS, followed by 635 nm laser irradiation (0.1 W/cm2, PDT); and (5) i.v. injection of HBCDs in PBS, followed by 635 nm laser irradiation (0.8 W/cm2, PDT + PTT). Meanwhile, the temperature changes of tumors were recorded with an IR thermal camera. It was discovered that the temperature of tumor in PDT + PTT group rapidly increased to 56.4 °C (>45 °C), which is sufficiently high to cause severe photothermal cell damage (Fig. 6 a and b) [15,79]. By contrast, in the PDT group, the tumor temperature only increased by 5.6 °C, indicating that HBCDs could only generate 1O2 for PDT at this condition. Subsequently, the volume changes of tumors were measured during the 14-d treatment (Fig. 6c and d). In the control groups of HBCDs and 635 nm laser irradiation, the tumors rapidly increased during the treatment period, suggesting no evident affection on the tumor growth. Meanwhile, in the PDT group, the mice showed an efficient treatment of tumor but could not be completely eliminated. The tumors were entirely inhibited with no recurrence after 14-day treatment in the synergistic PDT/PTT group, indicating better treatment efficacy compared with the PDT group. Moreover, the phototherapeutic efficacy was evaluated by H&E staining of tumor slices. The tumor tissue of PDT + PTT group was more serious damaged than that of the PDT group, whereas no apparent lesion was discovered in other control groups (Fig. 6e). Therefore, the anticancer efficacy of bifunctional PDT/PTT modality was more effective than that of the single PDT.

The body weights of mice (Fig. S5) were recorded during different treatments and no sudden change was found, demonstrating the low biotoxicity of HBCDs for in vivo treatment. The H&E stained slices of major organs, including heart, liver, spleen, lungs, and kidneys, were investigated for pathomorphology analysis to evaluate the toxicology of HBCDs. Compared with the control group without any treatment, no evident histological alterations were observed (Fig. S6). The results illustrated the low side effect and excellent biocompatibility of HBCDs in vivo.

In summary, we have successfully prepared a novel CD-based phototheranostic agent with HB as raw carbon source. The bimodal FL/PA imaging indicated that HBCDs could highly accumulate inside the tumor by EPR effect after i.v. injection. Moreover, the HBCDs could efficiently produce 1O2 and heat under 635 nm laser irradiation with 1O2 quantum yield of 0.38 and photothermal-conversion efficiency of 27.6%, respectively, thereby leading to an excellent anticancer efficacy of synergistic PDT/PTT. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated that the combination of PDT and PTT was more efficient than the single PDT modality in killing cancer cells. In addition, the H&E staining assay confirmed the excellent biocompatibility and low biotoxicity of HBCDs in vivo. A significant breakthrough in this work is the application of natural biomass as raw carbon source for preparation of the green, facile, and low-cost CDs with intrinsic phototheranostic characteristics for bimodel FL/PA imaging-guided synergistic PDT/PTT of cancer. More importantly, this study has effectively improved the shortcomings of complex synthetic route and high-cost of CD-based phototheranostic agent and provided a new approach for the synthesis of other carbon-based nanomaterials with natural biomass for cancer treatment. Although excellent anticancer efficacy achieved by the HBCDs, significant efforts should be further made to develop multifunctional CDs with significant potentials in clinical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51472252, 51572269, 61720106014), the Strategic Priority Research Program of the Chinese Academy of Sciences (Grant No. XDB17000000).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2018.05.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Figs. S1–S6

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photodynamic therapy: past, present and future
Functional nanomaterials for phototherapies of cancer
Synergic mechanisms of photothermal and photodynamic therapies mediated by photosensitizer/carbon nanotube complexes
Reactive oxygen species generating systems meeting challenges of photodynamic cancer therapy
Near-infrared light triggered photo-therapy, in combination with chemotherapy using magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots for effective cancer treating
A magnetofluorescent carbon dot assembly as an acidic H2O2-driven oxygenerator to regulate tumor hypoxia for simultaneous bimodal imaging and enhanced photodynamic therapy
Nanoparticle design strategies for enhanced anticancer therapy by exploiting the tumour microenvironment
Tumor microenvironment-triggered supramolecular system as an in situ nanotheranostic generator for cancer phototherapy
Chemical design and synthesis of functionalized probes for imaging and treating tumor hypoxia
Glycosylated porphyrins, phthalocyanines, and other porphyrinoids for diagnostics and therapeutics
Advancing porphyrin's biomedical utility via supramolecular chemistry
